The way I look at an account with Facebook is there's four key tiers which mirror the kind of customer journey towards the products or service. So a cold customer is one that you haven't hit before, they have no idea who you are. A warm customer is generally someone who hasn't really committed to visiting the site, but they've interacted with you in some way. A hot customer is really your prime customer at this point. They've visited the site, they've probably been in your cart page, reviewed some products, etc. They're the money makers at the bottom end of your conversion funnel. But then also existing customers, and this is one area where I think a lot of people leave money on the table is once they've made that first conversion is forgetting the fact that Facebook can also continue working generally alongside your CRM program to surface up promotions, etc., etc., to make sure that when they've made that first purchase, Facebook is pushing and pushing that lifetime value or engagement past that first purchase as well. So if I just break down the four parts of the funnel, in the top part we have the kind of cold part of what I call the funnel and the three main things I'm doing up here are testing with page post engagement, growing my social proof, using video views, because video, if you're not using it on Facebook, you absolutely have to, building up additional audiences and pushing out brand and product messages using video views.

## [09:08 - 12:08]

And website conversion, which for e-commerce in particular, is the key acquisition mechanic. You might be using mobile app install, you might be using EliteGen, but this is a typical funnel that I want to take you through. So in the cold part of the funnel, you're looking at broad interests, partner categories, behavior, lookalikes, etc. And now when I'm targeting cold audiences and doing my testing, generally I'm looking at Facebook and Instagram newsfeed only. From my experience, the money that goes in on a cold audience to audience network and messenger generally goes wasted. I do use those, but kind of further down the funnel just to get more impressions and more reach, but really at the cold level, I'm generally not doing that kind of stuff. And the messaging here is really trying to grab their intention and give them value. That kind of value exchange brings them into your funnel and then you can start messaging them afterwards direct response. So the warm part of the funnel is now about people that are engaged. So they could be engaged with your video, so targeting people that have viewed X percent of your video. It could be your fans on your page, it could be people that are on your email list, it could be people that have engaged with your ads, your posts, your Instagram, etc. So these are people who already know something about you and therefore the messaging that goes out to these people is one level down from knowing nothing about you to knowing something about you. But generally these people, and I'm excluding people who visit the site, they still need a bit of prompting to take that next step. And what I call it is nudge. They need a nudge. So going from cold to warm to hot to purchase, it's just one nudge at a time. I see a lot of people who run cold audience ads direct response and just want to generate it. So it works sometimes for low ticket items, 10, 20, $30 for example, but you start to step up and you need more of a commitment. You just need to nudge them one step along the journey. So this is what this structure kind of supports as well. And then going into kind of website retargeting, here generally what I'm trying to do now is give them more functional benefits and testimonials and fear of missing out, time limited offers, etc. And what I do is to create audiences based on how far down my sales funnel they've got. So if someone's just visited the landing page, not hit the cart, they're not as important to me as someone who's hit the cart, who's not as important to me as someone who's actually in the checkout. And what I do is balance my budget between those to make sure that I'm getting good performance and I'm able to maintain a good frequency. I want to get the spend as high as possible, but without alienating these people and overreaching them as well. So my retargeting for the campaigns I generally be using are conversion. The problem with conversion campaigns is it does need a good volume of audience to make it work. And actually, this is the only time I actually use a traffic objective as well. Because when you have those small audiences, you know that they've visited your site, they're interested in what you've got. You just want to grab them back.

## [12:08 - 15:10]

So it's worth thinking about your buying cycle and whether someone is likely to buy again if you're selling a product or service. Maybe there's an upsell or a cross sell that you can offer as well. ## [18:21 - 21:27]

And it was disapproved across all the different ads that it was running had a massive detrimental impact on the performance of the account. But because we're testing in the background, we had other creatives we could push in immediately. So it's just like there's always curve balls with Facebook. You don't know if it's the auction, policy team, XYZ. So having some thinking test, whether it's audiences or creatives is crucial to have that baseline for scaling as well. So what I want to take you through is what I call the graduation framework. This is something I've been using for many years to put some structure around the testing that I do. And this is something we use in the agency day in and day out and with clients. And this is what I train students up on as well. And there's two parts I want to take you through today around testing audiences and also testing creatives. So I call it the audience spotlighting strategy where you're testing different audiences out and trying to find the right audiences. And then the creative fishing strategy where you're dipping lots of creatives into the ocean. The analogy I give is the ocean being your audience, the fishing boats being your ad sets, and the hook being the creative. And you're dipping it into the ocean to see which ones the fish are biting on. And that's the kind of best analogy I can give on how to work creatives with Facebook to try and find the fish they're going to bite. So let's go into audience spotlighting. There's one part of this which is research I'll take you through and the second part is the implementation. In terms of research, there's five key areas I want to take you through on identifying who your audience should be. There are many others, but I think for the sake of time, these are the five that I recommend mostly out of the different strategies of audience research. Number one is audience insights. I'm hoping everyone here has used audience insights if you haven't, going to business manager into audience insights. There's a ton of stuff you can do with this. So the first thing I would do is like when we take on a new client, if they have a Facebook fan page and they already have fans, that's my first point of call. So on the left hand side, you can load up the fan page and start to get some insights as to who those fans are, what the demographics are, what kind of pages they like. And if they're in the US and there's enough information, you can get household and purchase data as well. And so for example, if I load up a fan page and in this one it says they're interested in some of these pages down here, I can then go and look at those pages and start building up a profile of interest to target as well, for example. You can also take your custom audience. So if you take an email list, you upload it up into Facebook, you can then reverse engineer that custom audience to find out who's actually within that custom audience. So to give you an example, I might take a cohort of my highest lifetime value customers and then bring them into audience insights and try and discover what that looks like in the back end. Or I might take customers that purchased in Q4, so Christmas period from Ecommerce site, who are they and are they different to people that buy in the other seasons, for example.

## [21:27 - 24:29]

So there's different ways you can cut this up, so you can kind of just get sense of what's going on. And then you can just do general research as well. So you can type in some interest on the left hand side into some demographics and kind of see what kind of interests and likes come out the back of that. So this is one of the easiest ways to do audience research as well. The second one, which I don't believe is available in all countries right now, but certainly is working in the UK, is doing page affinities. So in the previous example where I've gone through audience insights and I've found some pages that my potential customer is interested in, I would go and like that page and then I'd see recommendations of similar pages. So through using that, I can kind of go down the rabbit hole of seeing similar pages and start getting the sense for what kind of things are they posting up, what kind of images, how do they talk, and start to see if there's any other interest groups I can target as well. So that's the second one. The third one is probably one of my favorites is fans talking. So when you do build up your fans and you've got your page and people are interacting, people are liking your posts, etc. What I like to do is go and look at their profiles and see what their makeup is. What pages do they like? How do they talk and then you see what kind of things are they sharing? I'll give you an example. A couple of years ago, we expanded an e-commerce company into Italy and we ran some campaigns, we were doing OK, and we ran this, well, I ran this fan-stalking strategy. So what I would do is pick 25 to 50 fans. You could do 10, you could do 20 since I left you. And what I did was to tabulate those into a spreadsheet and looking at the common likes and things of interest across those different fans as well. And what I found was that there was one particular baby blender brand in Italy which had absolutely taken off. So it would blend the food, it would warm it up, you could take the container out and feed your baby immediately from it. So the real time saver was going really well in Italy and we targeted fans of that blender brand as well for the children's products. And that was like absolutely breakout for us, that worked really, really well. So that's just to give you an idea of how you can kind of look at your fans' interests as well and see if there's any other ideas for targeting. Then moving into, I guess, one of the easiest and most basic ways is actually through ad suggestions. If you do type in an interest, Facebook come up with recommendations related to that as well. And this is something which I like to do if I'm in a rush and I just want to get some ideas of what the interests are. One thing to look out for is if you do see any interest with a lower case, they're probably not very good interest to target. Facebook are trying to remove them, but the upper case interests are interests where Facebook has explicit data that someone is interested in diet food or fitness, because they're like to page related to it or like to post or something or written about it or something like that.

## [24:29 - 27:30]

If there's a lower case interest that comes up, it's an implied interest. Facebook doesn't have hard data that this interest is related to this user, but somewhere along the lines it thinks it might be interested. So for example, if I'm a friend of someone who's heavy into diet and fitness, I might come up as an implied interest because I'm a friend of someone that's got a hard interest in that as well. So if you are doing interest this way, make sure you look for the upper case interest as well. And then the last one I want to take you through are two external tools which I absolutely love. The first one is called FB Niche Finder. This is an amazing tool which actually goes into Facebook. You can type in interests, but it comes back with the reach as well. So within Facebook, if you look at the previous screen, Facebook doesn't tell you necessarily how many people are associated with each of these interests. So you have to go in one by one, load them up here or load them up sequentially and look at the differences, take them out, etc. But what Facebook FB Niche Finder does is if you type in a keyword, it will come back with the results and also suggestions and give you an idea of how big the reach is for those interests, which I think is super amazing as well. So that gives you an idea of audiences and interests that you can test out as well. And then the other one I also use is Google AdWords. So within Google AdWords, in a similar way, you can type in keywords, but then come up with related words that people are actually searching for. And then you can come back into Facebook and see if those interests exist as well. So there are just some ideas on audience research. The next part of spotlighting is actually going ahead and testing. So when I'm testing audiences out, I run them as page post engagement campaigns. The reason being that when you jump straight into website conversion or leadger or mobile app install, you're asking the algorithm to find people who are ready to buy. And so you may lose out on some learnings on whether actually this audience or this creative blend is good enough. So that's the reason why I go into page post engagement is because the pool of people in that audience is going to be bigger. And you'll capture people who are in the buying mode as well as people are not in the buying mode. But you'll definitely get the interest back on whether the audience and the creative blend works as well. And I'll kind of go through, in the next slide, the metrics I'm looking at for that. When I'm testing my audiences within a page post engagement campaign, I'm trying to keep one audience per ad set. So it's either one interest or one behavior or behavior interest pair or lookalike or custom audience, so that I can kind of see exactly which interest is working. And then I'll run a range of creatives within that to try and make sure I'm capturing the right message as well. So for example, if I'm doing health and nutrition, and I've got a range of audience interests and lookalikes being targeted, the creatives might be a range of different images and copy related to different parts of health and nutrition.

## [27:30 - 30:31]

So if I'm selling a health smoothie or a food supplement or something, I might try well being weight loss bodybuilding as my messaging to just try and find the message that resonates best with this audience as well. Once I find a winning combo of audience and creative, I'll then graduate that into my main campaign. So if that's a lead gen or a website conversion, I'll then graduate that up. If you're doing the website conversion campaign, what I generally do is I create the ads in a dummy website conversion campaign objective. And then I share the post ID up into my page post engagement. And then if it works, I then share that post ID back into website conversion. So any social proof that's been built up, you can carry that forward as well. It's also far quicker to create ads as well. And also if you are testing interests in your page post engagement, if you find interest at work, it's better to group them up when you start to go into scale out because bigger audiences are better. But I would also group interest by their relative compatibility. So if I've got interests related to well-being and I've got one related to bodybuilding, I personally would probably keep that separate because the creators within that will be separate. So it's based on how I'm messaging it as to how and when I group up as well. So the next slide I want to show is an example of this in action. This particular account, we were looking for interests related to health and well-being. So these are UK brands, some of them are more international. So Holland Barrett, Vita Coco, Vita Bot and Biotics, they're health supplement brands. Hello Fresh, Gusto, our food home delivery, Reddy Mill delivery, Stabiletics and Sweaty Betty are fashion brands. And actually we were testing a group of these to see which ones would resonate best with our different audiences. When I'm doing testing like this, I run the budgets at £10 or $10 per ad set. It doesn't have to be a huge amount. If your budget is only $10 a day, then run one, maybe split that into two fives. For this client, we had a bit more to spend per day and I've split that out into different interests. And what I'm looking at, so within this example, the account average for a click-through rate on our website conversion was about half a percent. And the cost per click was £1.50-ish. So I'm looking across these range of interests and seeing which ones are working, which are not. Clearly we've got three here that just did not resonate whatsoever. And we've got a couple that did work. So with this example, Holland Barrett, Gusto then got graduated to a website conversion campaign. And in particular, with Holland Barrett, it started to work a lot better and we got better cost per click. Now, you might be wondering why run it in a page post engagement and look at click-through rate on CPC and not cost per engagement. The reason is, when you run a page post engagement campaign, Facebook is trying to deliver it to people who are likely to engage with the post. And that's generally a like, share or comment.

## [30:31 - 33:33]

When I'm doing this testing, I'm not interested in those metrics. I want to know how many people are actually clicking through and clicking out of the ad into the website. So that's why what I tend to do is look at click-through rates, because even though it's a PPE ad, if people are actually clicking through, that's a strong indicator that this ad is working for them. And so the click-through rate is my key metric here. The second thing I'm also looking at is, for example, with e-commerce or if there's some kind of sales funnel off the back of it, is once they do click out, how far are they getting down the funnel? You will find that in some cases people will go all the way in purchase as well. But I'm looking at predominantly free commerce, how many people are actually viewing the products and actually adding to cart, because that's a really strong indicator alongside click-through rate. And at the cart, the products and the kind of creative and the audience are working quite well. In this example, this is Elite Gen for a digital product. We tested four audiences out and they were a combination of one custom audience and three interest-based audiences, where we did a lot of testing and then grouped interest together. And clearly you can see here, for example, with our click-through rates, we had big differences in the kind of good and bad for us. And also when I'm running the video, and this was purely a video campaign, I'm looking at the cost for a 10-second view. So how much is it costing for me to get people to view 10 seconds of the video to find my winners as well? So although there wasn't a massive difference here, certainly based on consistent results, we were getting $0.03 and less from our engagement of those videos. And so that became our kind of max. So as long as they're all there about, then that was a winner for us. And we'd graduate that to our website conversion campaign as well. The second part of the graduation framework is creative fishing strategy. This is about, so, you know, where you've looked previously at audiences, it's now focusing on the creative. Now for an account that has a bit of history, what it would generally do is if I'm testing audiences, I'm testing my best creative in those audiences. However, if you don't have creative, then this is the other side of it as well. If you're starting out and you've got nothing, then you just have to keep iterating creators and audiences until you find the right blend. It's easier to pin down the best audience and then start creating more tests for your creatives. And this is kind of talking about that. So this is just focusing on the ads. So it's the format, the copy, the visual, call to action, etc. I again use page post engagement to test the ads, but it can be tested with video views, lead gen, mobile app install as well. And what you're doing is splitting out the test budget from your core budget. And that's where the graduation comes through. So for me, I'm testing in page post engagement and graduating that through as well. The, what I try to do is try to limit the variance as well. So if I've got one version of an ad, I might change the image for another one or keep the same image and change the copy because you want to know exactly what's working there as well. Again, kind of run from as low as $5, $10 a day.

## [33:33 - 36:34]

I would generally test three ads per ad set, see which ones start to get more traffic. If they're not working, I'd pause them. If they are, then if they're good enough, then I'd graduate them to my main campaign. So you've always got new ads coming in to be tested. If the ad is not ready to be moved into your main campaign, then I have a kind of winning ad set set up where all my winners for page post engagement are stored. They're running $5 a day, $10 a day, and they're ready to be moved up as and when I need creative as well. One thing to kind of bear in mind is I use the kind of star method of testing. So set up test, analyze and repeat. The most important part is analysis and being able to feed that into your next test as well. So you're kind of creating an ongoing cycle to keep churning out some great creative as well. In terms of the creative set up, so, you know, you're probably hearing this a lot. I'm not sure if you're talking about Avatar, who you're talking to, but I've kind of extended it further, you know, walking there should stop them online. What kind of words are they using? What visuals do they respond to? For example, if you're targeting health and fitness or gyms or anything like that, then going to some of these forums of these gyms and bodybuilding, et cetera, how are they talking? What kind of images are they sharing and get sense for kind of how you can replicate that in your ads as well. Right copy based on the creative you're talking to. So if it's a bodybuilder or someone that's losing, trying to lose weight, there's a different way of appealing and motivating for them as well. Test one format at a time. So, you know, if it's a video, then test that with a range of thumbnails and copy, for example. And also remove emotion and guesswork as well by using this approach. There's been many occasions where I've written copy, I think it's good, or I've selected images, I think it's great, and it hasn't worked. And you have to sometimes just let go of your ego and let the data decide as well. The other thing to mention is the first 500 impressions matters at Facebook. That's when it decides whether the creative is going to work well in the auction or not. That's also the point of where relevance score kicks in as well. And I think there's a reason for Facebook to do that because they don't want ads to continually be in served if the relevance is really low, the ads are irrelevant, and the feedback is generally not good. However, the caveat I'd give is it's not always the ads' faults if it doesn't work out. Just sometimes you might end up with those first 500 impressions being 500 people in your audience that are just not right as well. So, if you really do feel the ad should have worked better and hit 500,000 impressions, literally just duplicate it and try it again. Sometimes that helps it kick in as well. One thing I also wanted to reflect on is when you're thinking about creative and your copy and your images as well, is meso-sparic of needs. If you've not seen it, it's looking at our human basic needs. And I try to visualise that when I'm thinking about the person that I'm copyrighting for. So, I do copyrighting for the ads.

## [36:34 - 39:40]

And the lower you are on that pyramid and the more basic your needs, the easier it is to understand for the customer. So, if you're selling something regarding food or water or sleep or anything like that, people know what that is. They do that every day. And so, you don't have to explain, this is what food is, you put it in your mouth, you chew it, etc. It's just a given. So, you need to convince them why your food is better, for example. And actually, it's harder to convince someone with something so basic as well, because it needs a bit more explanation. As you go up the hierarchy, it's things that people move away from being the kind of basics to being things that people want to aspire to. So, creativity, problem-solving, acceptance of facts, self-esteem, contents. There are a lot of things that come across in digital products, for example. It's harder for the customer to understand that. And here, it needs a lot more storytelling. So, I work with people who have been dropshipping, for example, and a lot of the ads are, here's my product, you should buy it, here's some tick lists, you'll love it. That just doesn't work with high-ticket items or items that are not the norm, for example. So, if you're selling coaching programs on confidence, for example, you need to be doing storytelling, you need to take people through a journey, and it takes a bit longer to convince people to trust you and then to be engaging with you as well. So, this is an example of running a creative strategy for a given ad set. So, this ad set here, this screen, I just want to illustrate how I do analysis. This particular ad set, it's a look-alike, we've done some testing. And what I would do is look at the average cost-per-click-and-click-through rate for the ad set and then decide which ads are working well within that. So, here, for example, this 1.67% click-through rate for this ad would suggest that this ad is doing really, really well. But actually, when you look at the downstream metrics, actually, I'm getting purchases from this one as well. Now, granted it's had more traffic, I want to kind of put more into this, but do have a look at your downstream traffic as well. This one here, for example, it's had a 1.37% click-through rate, slightly lower spend. There are already three people that have purchased on a really good cost-per-purchase. And to get that kind of level from PagePrice Engagement is a great indicator that this is going to work in a website conversion as well. So, when I'm kind of coming up with creative ideas, I'm looking at my formats and creating variants of formats, and then I'll have a few copy variants as well. And you can see here, for example, with four format variants and three copy variants, I've got 12 ads I can test. So, quite quickly, you can test out different formats as well. I would also mention that Facebook rolled out dynamic content optimization in the last year as well. And that allows you to do this kind of testing at the website conversion level. So, you can take different elements of headlines, of images, etc. And Facebook will self-optimize that in the background. That's also quite self-limiting, because you don't get a ton of data on which variant got the traffic, etc.

So, it's harder to dissect why something worked. But this is a quicker way of just running with lots of variants within one ad, within your main website conversion campaign. For me personally, I prefer to use this approach with PagePrice Engagement. So, this is another example of where you need to just be careful of the metrics you're looking at. So, what I wanted to highlight here is these are some ads that we are running for a hair product company. And we had some real big breakout performances in terms of click-through rate. Close to 3%, we had one at 6%, and the cost of click was insanely low. Now, it would be great to push this out and say, hey, look, I've got 6%. Click-through rate on e-commerce and 0.1 cost of click, aren't I great? Well, actually, what happened was it was a bit click-vated, and the copy really got lots of people to click through. But they just weren't progressing past the landing page and going into the cart. So, that's just something to bear in mind that when you are doing your testing, yes, if you want people to come off of Facebook into your site, but you want them to come for the right reason as well. You want them to know what your product is and to engage them on that as well. So, just quickly to recap on testing. We've got 15 minutes, so I just really want to get into that last section. Data in the insights, make sure you've set up the right reports in the ads manager based on your objectives. So, for example, when I'm doing testing with PPE, I'm focusing on CPC and CTR. But I'm also looking for the downfall metrics as well, and potentially also my video metrics. So, make sure you've got those reviews set up in ads manager as well, so you can do quick analysis. Document your learnings. There's a Trello growth board, there's a bit of a link there. I think you'll see it later on as well once the final presentation has been shared out. I use a Trello board to kind of queue up the test and make sure that I'm keeping track of those as well. There's also a list on that second link of creative apps and techniques. So, it's actually on the side there in the comment section. There's a ton of apps to help with your creatives as well. So, really some really good free tools. There's no excuse to innovate on your creatives. Even if you don't have budget, just take your camera phone and start playing around with ideas as well. Give enough time and money to your testing, but make sure you're cutting early as well. So, generally the benchmarks I'm looking at are at least a thousand to two thousand impressions, ideally five thousand impressions to evaluate the test, or no more than $50, maybe $100, depending on whether I'm getting enough data back as well. Try and test one audience every fortnight at least, and make sure you're testing at least one creative every week. To me, I'm doing it like literally every day, but it depends on how much throughput you've got. Because when your creatives start to fail, when your audiences start to fail, that's not the time to be worrying about new audiences that are creative. You want them queued up and ready in the background as well. And also, the higher the ticket price, the longer the journey is going to be. So, you need to be a lot better at storytelling for an item that's selling at, you know,

## [42:41 - 45:41]

$100,000 to $500,000 plus, and then you work for an item that's selling $20,000 to $30,000. You can be a bit more direct in that response as well. And the last point was just to remove emotion from your testing, and make sure you're taking a data-driven approach as well. So, the final section is scaling. And, you know, this is the big golden ticket, I guess, that people are looking for, and how do you scale on Facebook? And there's so many different approaches. People have their own preferences. The way that I use scaling and the way that I run this in the agency is to create consistency. So, I don't necessarily go for the blockbuster results, but I go for something that's sustainable and that's going to maintain performance as well. And so, within the scaling part, I'm going to talk about three things here. So, one is mindset. The other is the actual auction, and then there's scaling strategies. And then there's the mindset. So, going back some 10, maybe 11 years now, I actually did foreign exchange trading, so trading currency pairs. And I think, honestly, that made a huge difference for me in understanding how Facebook works as well. The Facebook auction is unpredictable. There's a lot of speculation that goes in in terms of what's going to work, what's not. There's profit, there's loss, there's risk and reward. You have to look at the trends. You have to look at when to cut things, when to push things harder. There's external market forces, and you will probably lose money before you profit. That's exactly how day trading works. That's exactly how Facebook works as well. So, when it comes to mindset, it's knowing when to break your losses, but then also when to scale up and when the right time is to do that as well. Then, kind of talking about the auction itself, some of you might have seen this before. This is a graphic that Facebook shared of what the makeup is of the auction itself. This is an important part of scaling so that you understand why scaling works and why it doesn't as well. The advertiser bid, that comes down to your audience, how much you're actually bidding, your ad set history, your objective, your optimised pixel. There's many factors that come into what comes up into your final bid. Then your estimated action rate is related to your funnel, your endpoint, but also your pixel that's been optimised against for website conversion, or your overall performance for an ad set as well. There's things that Facebook are looking at here, but also something that also comes into play here is your kind of website history, pixel history, etc. So, there's a kind of mini formula that goes on here as well. Then the user value, so that comes down to the obvious ones, a relevant score, but also the history of the ad and the ad set. But also don't discount the impact of landing page speed, the sales funnel experience and user history as well. So, with the pixel that you install on your site, yes, it gives you data access to what people are doing on your site, but it gives Facebook access to every single user on every single site that's using Facebook. So, for example, Facebook will know exactly how that user responds on your website versus your competitor

## [45:41 - 48:41]

and which one it's likely to convert more on, and therefore it will evaluate that in the bid and serve up the creative that's going to perform best for the user. So, that's something just bear in mind is user experience is so critical, and it's not just on Facebook, it's also off-site as well. So, keep an eye on your bounce rate, your landing page load time, your funnel conversion rates, because Facebook take all of those into account as well. To scout predictably, there's so many things that need to come together. So, this is just a real high-level bullet point list, but your pixel build and what kind of events you're sending back, your avatar and how well your audience and ad set has been set up, your campaign build and what kind of objectives it's using, the content, what kind of content you're using at different parts of your funnel, your reporting and being able to make a decision on the right data, your testing strategy, your scaling strategy, site speed, landing page, but also your kind of sales funnel, especially for e-commerce, your upsell, cross-sell, lifetime value as well. So, there are all things that come into account when managing your bid in the auction as well. One thing I do want to touch on is where I see many people failing with scaling is understanding how audiences work in Facebook and how the auction finds people as well. So, in a given ad set, let's say you've got 10 million people there, when you're running a website conversion campaign, Facebook is isolating the people that are more likely to purchase if that's what you're optimizing for. It will not be 10 million people and that's an important thing to differentiate, that it's pulling out the most likely people that are going to convert. It could be 500,000, it could be a million, it could be 5 million. But the point there is when you look at 10 million audiences and you say, no, I've got a huge scope to grow here, you're playing with a smaller pool. And actually, when you look at all the different objectives that apply to any given audience, Facebook looks at it in this kind of way. So, there's a group of people that are likely to buy, there's a group of people that are likely to view a video that will click, that will engage, and they're all your objectives. But I really want you to take away that those objectives have huge crossover as well. So, for example, if you're running a traffic campaign in a website conversion campaign, there will be people in your traffic campaign that are also purchasers. It doesn't mean you have to use that campaign. The Purchasers campaign will give you better performance. But as I went through before in my retargeting funnel, with those smaller audiences, I generally try and test the traffic campaign alongside the website conversion campaign to see where I'm getting the best performance. And sometimes the traffic campaign performs better. Oftentimes, they work well together as well. So, it's just something to bear in mind. And the same applies to video views. If you're doing any kind of campaigns and you run video views alongside your call, whether it's LitlyJer or website conversion, often it complements your activity as well because you're attracting some of the buyers in there as well. And the same again for page post engagement. And this is why when you're doing testing in page post engagement or just running social proof, you will also generate sales.

## [48:41 - 51:43]

It doesn't mean that Facebook absolutely know that these people are going to buy or they're not going to buy. This is about just understanding the kind of crossover that's as wide. And then also the final point on this is how people cross between those different things as well. So, I might be a purchaser for one given type of product, but I'm probably just going to view videos for another product. So, one given person can be in many of these audiences at any moment in time as well. It's dependent on your product, your targeting, relevant score, etc., etc. in terms of how that works. Then the kind of final piece on mindset is thinking about when you are scaling up and you're optimizing, how much risk do you want to put in against how much reward do you want to get out? So, most people when they're starting out are in this kind of bottom quadrant. But if you want to stay safe, then you move up into this quadrant where the reward is fairly high, but you're not increasing your bids and your budgets too fast. I see a lot of people that end up here. So, they've seen some success and they're spending $10, maybe even $100 a day, start to see some good success and they want to scale it super fast. And then things just fall apart. For me, I think the better approach is to take fewer risks, but scale it up in a bit more of a controlled way, where you kind of take a step up from, I guess, the beginner state to taking a bit more control, and then you go into the high roller states where you built up the confidence in the data, you've got pool of creatives, you've got audiences that are tested, and then you build up your confidence to be able to scale a lot faster as well, and absolutely to avoid this area where possible. So, now let's go into the scaling strategies. So, there's a few different ways that I like to scale. The two primary methods are vertical and horizontal scaling. So, essentially vertical scaling, as the name suggests, is taking your winning asset and scaling it upwards. And so, if you've got $50 a day, I would scale it to 20% up to 60% to 72%, 8%, 60%, and 3%. So, if I scale that five times over five days, I've made an increment from 50% to 103%. So, I've doubled my budget essentially. And it's not by any means a bad strategy, but obviously if you want to get from $50 to $500 to $5,000, it's a lot slower to get to, which is where the horizontal strategy comes in. So, when you take your winning asset, you don't increase the budget there necessarily, but you make duplicates of that asset, and you make one change within the asset and then see how that one performs. So, for example, when I'm running this, I'll make a duplication, I'll make a duplication, change one thing, see if I can make any changes there. And I'll talk you through kind of how I run the detail of those as well. So, vertical scaling strategy is about maintaining the same audience and the build on that asset. The history of performance is kept at that level. So, when Facebook talked about 50 conversions and performance of the asset, it's all at the asset level. So, when you're using the vertical scaling strategy, it maintains that history. The risk is that you could unsettle a good performer. Scaling is also slower.

## [51:43 - 54:44]

And also, generally, I wouldn't recommend doing more than 20% every other day until you're confident in the performance and the stats as well. Personally, when I'm confident in the performance, I do 20% twice a day. First thing early in the morning based on yesterday's performance, and then one kind of mid to late afternoon based on the day's performance as well. But I'd only do that when you're super confident. I also do 10% reductions when I see a performance drop. That sends Facebook back a signal that something's not working and often it kickstarts better performance as well. Also, when you're scaling up, beware your CPCs generally end up increasing. It's also an opportunity to look at creative refresh as well, where creatives are working, stop working, and keep an eye on delivery insights. So, within Ads Manager, you can have a look at the ads at performance to look at audience overlap, look at saturation of those audiences as well. Often when you start to scale up your spend, you start to reach more people and your audience can become saturated and you need to refresh your creatives a lot more frequently as well. And keep an eye on your key lead metrics, whether it's CPA, CPL, or CPR, or cost-riping store, and also CPC as you start to scale up to make sure that you're still maintaining your profitability. And what I also do is the staircase scaling tactic with this as well. So, as I mentioned before, I wouldn't increase the budget more than once every two days, 20% to be on the safe side. But if you get more confidence, you can be a lot more aggressive and do two per day, and that depends on where you are with the risk and reward. But then also think about punishing the outset if it's not performing as well. And so, to give you an example, this is one that I ran recently. And this is to give you an idea of the scale where we went, I think, from like $3 to $500 a day to $5,000 in a matter of 10 days and using these kind of strategies as well. And so, what does the V-scaling look like? Actually, if you look in this kind of way and doing 20% increments, doing it twice a day on some occasions, you can get a decent spend quite quickly. And it's a fairly safe way of doing it. It's a slower way of scaling, but it's certainly doable to reach good numbers quite quickly, especially when you are hitting kind of three-figure spends as well. So, just to give an example here, on the 26th, there was a 20% increase, another one here, et cetera, all the way down. And what you're looking at is the compound impact of each of your increases. So, from the 26th to the 31st of January, we increased the budget by 300%. So, from 500 a day to nearly 1500, which, you know, if I said to you, I can do that in five days, you might be like, oh, there might be a risk there. But when you're doing it in this kind of way, it's a bit more of a controlled risk than duplicating and going from 500 to 1500 in a single day as well. Now, onto the kind of horizontal scaling strategy. With this one, what you're doing is you're not touching the original asset, and this example is at $50. But you're making duplicates of it as well. So, generally what I'm doing is making, let's say, it depends on what budget I'm aiming for.

## [54:44 - 57:47]

If I want to go from 50 to 250 or 500, then I might split it into multiple $50 budgets or might create $100, $200 budget. What I'm looking at here is am I consistently getting good performance on this asset? If so, then I'm going to be comfortable duplicating that. I would duplicate exactly the same asset there. But what I'm doing is when I'm duplicating, I make one straight copy. And then for the other ones, I'm just changing one variable at a time. So, for here, if it's an automatic bid, I'll introduce manual bid, and then I might do a different manual bid. So, generally I'd start at three times my cost per acquisition target, and then I might do a five times, I might do a two times bid, I might do a max versus average or target versus cap as well. So, it's just changing one variable at a time. And then I might also look at placements and devices as well. So, if you see, for example, this winning asset is having great performance on Instagram, break it out, give it a bit more budget, and see if you can actually squeeze more performance out of it as well. So, this is a far quicker way of scaling up, but it comes with risk as well, because every duplication you make, you're resetting the learning. You need to hit your conversion, ideally your 50 conversions per asset. In practice, I think 25 to 30 plus is generally okay, but you're resetting the learning each time as well. This method does come with its risk as well. Also, it works better with bigger audiences, because just making duplications, you could end up competing with yourself for those impressions for those customers as well. So, ideally, if your audiences are one to two million plus, it generally works better though as well. So, just going on to the final few slides with one mind at a time as well. So, this is just a real quick example where we identified one winner, which was below our $35 CPA target, made a strict duplication, came back with 40. And so, we started to scale those out, for example. And here, for example, you can see the budgets that ended up being in place. So, we kept the original budget on our original ad set. You can see how the other ones scaled up. So, this one, for example, the maxbid 9s was performing really well. And what I actually did with this is, whilst I duplicated it, so I did the hscaling, I then did the vertical scaling on top, and I was really aggressive with that. So, I was doing 20% twice a day. And what that meant was I was de-risking my call campaign and carrying all the risk here. So, if it went completely balls up and it just didn't work out, then it doesn't matter. I'd pause that and go back and maybe make another duplicate and start again. So, that's just one way of testing different variables here. New landing page, for example, different bids to see if you can squeeze more performance out of it. So, that's a kind of example of the strategy that I use on vertical scaling, my good performing ad set, and then horizontal scaling that to see if I can squeeze more performance out and scale a lot quicker, and then vertically scaling any of these women as well, so increasing 20% on any of these that are doing well as well.

## [57:47 - 1:00:47]

And then the kind of inverse supplies as well. So, if you go H first, then you can do V, and you can H. You can keep on going in this way as well. Let me just skip through that. So, one thing I wanted to kind of focus on here is when you're doing CPA optimization, there's only two metrics that you need to absolutely focus on, your cost per click and your conversion rate. So, here in this example, the cost per click is $2.03. Conversion rate is 6%. There's your calculation to work out CPAs. So, if for example you want to get that down to 25 or 20, you're going to have to increase the conversion rate or drop your CPC. And in practice, it's hard to do both at the same time. So, CPC is all about what's on the Facebook side, or mostly about that. And then conversion rate is a combination of that, plus your off-site activity as well. So, when you're trying to kind of look at optimizing, let's say e-commerce, 6% is actually really good for an e-commerce conversion rate, then there's probably more work that needs to be done to bring this CPC down to half, if not less of that. So, then you focus a lot more on your activity and your time on the Facebook side of things. Same goes for ROAS. So, if you're an e-commerce, ROAS is probably your prime metric. ROAS is a calculation of CPA and AOV, and it's the ratio between the both of those. And once we've kind of looked at CPA, then AOV is actually more about your merchandising, and the higher you can get your AOV, the higher you get your ROAS, then you can drop your CPA as well. And the final, final part is rules and optimization. So, I'm a huge fan of using rules and optimizations, but what I would encourage you to do is don't jump straight into rules, understand how they do, how they work manually, make them work for you manually, and then start to introduce rules. I've been doing Facebook for five, six years now, and I still get rules wrong. It's so easy to get them wrong because what you think it's doing, sometimes it doesn't do. So, for example, I've had occasions where I said if CPA is below a certain number, then cut it. Well, actually, if you've regenerated the zero sales, the CPA is zero, so it will just cut it without even spending anything. So, you just have to make sure you're getting the rules right when you're doing this as well. I use a tool called Rebuildbar, absolutely love it. Facebook had their internal rules engine, so you can do some basic testing there. But, tall aside, it's more about just making sure you understand how these rules work and applying them yourselves, and automation and testing them and monitoring them before letting them run away and ruining your account as well. So, a few examples. Pools bad performing ads based on threshold metric at 1 p.m. So, if they haven't met their cost per acquisition target or cost per install, cut it. Or conversely, if you've got paused ad and performance comes in within three days, then unpause it. So, for example, based on the attribution window, you might generate some clicks today, but the sales come through tomorrow. So, that ad actually did perform well. You want to restart that one. You can do vscaling, so increase budget 20% every morning if the last three days performance has been good. Or you can do hate scaling, duplicate the ad set, or ads based on performance.

## [1:00:47 - 1:03:48]

And also, you can do alerts if there are all paused ads in an ad set. This is something I've come across recently where we've been pausing ads that have not been performing and all of a sudden there's no ads running in the ad set. So, you can create alerts for things like that as well. So, final slide, quick summary. Being in control of your data, understand the pulse of your account. What does good look like? What times do good look like? Those good performance come through. Build your account structuring your sales funnel to support your business objective and the content you've got. Continually test everything. Become a storyteller with your content. Engage and get people to take interest in your ad and really build on that engagement as well. Use PPE for low risk, low cost testing. Document the testing. So, have a look at the Trello board. Make sure you're testing at least one audience every two weeks and at least doing creative testing every week, if not every few days. Use some of the links here to download some creative apps and start playing with ideas. Cut ads that are not working. It's right and move on. Don't get emotionally attached to any ads. Just keep on testing. Just keep changing one thing and test it and change it up again. Have your craters queued up so that when you are scaling, one of the biggest barriers I find is when you're scaling by any magnitude is having enough craters to be running in the background. And that's where the graduation framework combined with the scaling works really, really well together as well. Scale vertically 20% every other day to stay safe. Scale horizontally to go faster and make one change per headset as well. And then vertically scale those duplicates as well.
